hack.practice: Lab both: line with 'password X' + 'login' (shared) vs line with 'login local' + 'username X secret Y' (per-user). Observe: shared = 1 prompt. Local = 2 prompts (username then password). Shows the difference in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam staple. login local activates local DB. Always pair with username X secret Y. Without login local, local users are dead config.

5.3.f.info:
Cisco IOS uses privilege levels 0-15 to control what commands a user can execute. Higher numbers = more commands allowed. Users are assigned a level via username X privilege N secret Y, and entering priv-exec mode can elevate to a higher level if authorized.

Default levels:

  • Level 0 — minimal commands: logout, enable, disable, help, exit. Essentially useless alone.
  • Level 1 (user EXEC) — default user mode (the > prompt). Includes show (most), ping, traceroute, telnet, ssh. Cannot configure.
  • Level 15 (privileged EXEC) — full admin (the # prompt). All commands, including configure terminal, reload, erase, copy.

Levels 2-14 are custom — by default they have no distinct command set, but admins can assign specific commands to specific levels using:

Router(config)# privilege exec level 5 show running-config
Router(config)# privilege exec level 5 configure terminal
Router(config)# username helpdesk privilege 5 secret Pass123

This gives user 'helpdesk' the ability to view running-config and enter config mode (but not execute every command in config mode — needs further granting).

How levels relate to enable/enable secret:

  • By default, enable command elevates to level 15 using enable secret.
  • enable secret level 5 SomePass creates a secret for level 5 specifically. User enters enable 5 and supplies that password.
  • A user assigned privilege 15 on their username line goes straight to level 15 on login — no separate enable step needed.

Exam patterns:

  • Level 15 = full admin.
  • Level 1 = basic user EXEC (default for new users unless privilege specified).
  • Level 0 = severely limited.
  • Levels 2-14 = custom (need manual privilege exec level commands to be useful).
  • username X privilege 15 secret Y + login local = user goes straight to # on login.

hack.memory: Levels 0-15. 0=almost nothing. 1=basic user EXEC (>). 15=full admin (#). 2-14=custom (need privilege commands). 'username X privilege 15 secret Y' + 'login local' = instant admin on login. Exam: priv 1 = show only, priv 15 = god mode.
hack.practice: Lab: create 'username testuser privilege 1 secret Pass' and 'username admin privilege 15 secret Pass'. Log in as testuser — only show commands work. Log in as admin — can enter 'configure terminal'. Observe the difference.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests levels 0, 1, 15 specifically. Know 1 = basic user, 15 = admin. Custom 2-14 are rarely used but exam may mention them.

5.3.g.info:
service password-encryption is a global configuration command that applies Type 7 Vigenere encoding to all plaintext (Type 0) passwords currently in the running configuration and all future passwords set with the password keyword. It provides a weak obfuscation layer to prevent casual shoulder-surfing but is NOT real encryption.

Syntax:

Router(config)# service password-encryption

That's it — no parameters. Once enabled, IOS walks the running config, converts any Type 0 passwords to Type 7, and continues doing so for any new plaintext passwords.

What it affects:

  • enable password cisco → enable password 7 0822455D0A16
  • line con 0 + password cisco → password 7 0822455D0A16
  • username bob password cisco → username bob password 7 0822455D0A16

What it does NOT affect:

  • enable secret — already Type 5/8/9 hash. Unchanged.
  • username X secret Y — already hashed. Unchanged.
  • Type 7 passwords that are already encoded — stays Type 7.

Reversibility disclaimer: Type 7 uses Cisco's proprietary Vigenere cipher with a fixed, publicly known key. Any Type 7 string can be reversed to plaintext in milliseconds using free online decoders or Python snippets. It stops a non-technical observer from reading a password over your shoulder, but provides zero defense against anyone with Google access.

Effect of disabling with no service password-encryption: previously encrypted passwords STAY encrypted (IOS doesn't decrypt them back to Type 0). Future passwords entered with password keyword will be stored as Type 0 plaintext again.

Best-practice role: enable it as a safety net, but treat it as defense-in-depth layer, not as a real security control. Primary hardening = use secret keyword for real hashing.

hack.memory: service password-encryption = converts Type 0 (plaintext) to Type 7 (Vigenere, REVERSIBLE). Weak — stops shoulder-surf only. Does NOT affect enable secret or username secret (already hashed). Safety net, not real security.
hack.practice: Lab: 'line con 0' → 'password cisco'. show run = Type 0 'cisco'. 'service password-encryption'. show run = Type 7 hex. Copy the hex, paste to online Cisco Type 7 decoder, get 'cisco' back. That's the reversibility.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4 + Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 + 27. Exam staple. Know it produces Type 7, affects Type 0 passwords only, and is REVERSIBLE (not real security).

5.3.g.info:
Type 7 is reversible. It uses Cisco's proprietary Vigenere cipher with a fixed, publicly known key that has been reverse-engineered since the 1990s. Anyone with the Type 7 string and a simple tool can recover the original plaintext password in under a second.

Why it's trivially broken:

  • Vigenere is a 16th-century cipher, not modern cryptography.
  • Cisco's implementation uses a fixed key: dsfd;kfoA,.iyewrkldJKDHSUB.
  • Same password always produces the same (or very similar) Type 7 string, so patterns are detectable.
  • Tools include: getpass.pl, cisco7decrypt.py, many online web decoders. All take the hex string and return plaintext instantly.

Example:

password 7 0822455D0A16

Paste that hex into any online Cisco Type 7 decoder → returns cisco.

Why Cisco still supports it: backward compatibility. Older devices can still be configured with Type 7. Modern devices accept Type 7 but admins should prefer Type 5/8/9 via secret.

When is Type 7 acceptable?

  • Labs and non-production environments.
  • Temporary masking for over-the-shoulder protection.
  • Legacy compatibility with old devices/images.

When is Type 7 unacceptable?

  • Any production device storing passwords.
  • Any config that might be backed up, emailed, or attached to tickets.
  • Any compliance environment (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX require strong password storage).

What to use instead:

  • enable secret — Type 5 default, Type 8/9 available.
  • username X secret Y — hashed.
  • AAA server authentication — offload passwords to RADIUS/TACACS+ where they're managed centrally.

hack.memory: Type 7 = TRIVIALLY REVERSIBLE Vigenere cipher with fixed key. Free online decoders return plaintext instantly. It's obfuscation, not encryption. Prefer Types 5, 8, 9 via the 'secret' keyword.
hack.practice: Copy any Type 7 hex string (like 0822455D0A16) to an online Cisco Type 7 decoder — watch it return the plaintext instantly. This makes the weakness visceral.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam knows Type 7 is reversible. Questions often contrast Type 7 (weak) with Type 5 (one-way). Always prefer hash-based approaches.

5.3.g.info:
The universal Cisco password rule: use the secret keyword wherever it's offered, never password. secret produces a one-way hash (Type 5/8/9) that cannot be reversed. password produces Type 0 plaintext (or Type 7 with service password-encryption — still reversible).

Where to prefer secret:

  • enable secret MyPass — NOT enable password MyPass.
  • username X secret Y — NOT username X password Y.
  • (Line passwords like password cisco under line vty don't have a secret alternative — but you can avoid them entirely by using login local and defining usernames with secret.)

Why it matters:

  • Config backups to TFTP/SCP/Git contain password strings. Hashes are safe(r); plaintext is leaked credentials.
  • Show running-config output can be sent in tickets, screenshots, docs. Hashes are fine; plaintext is not.
  • Tools and APIs that ingest config can store passwords. Hashes are safer.
  • Compliance frameworks require hashed password storage.

The full hardened pattern:

Router(config)# enable algorithm-type scrypt secret AdminPass!2026
Router(config)# username admin privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypt secret AdminPass!2026
Router(config)# line vty 0 15
Router(config-line)# login local
Router(config-line)# transport input ssh
Router(config)# service password-encryption

Breakdown: enable secret with Type 9, username with Type 9, VTY uses local auth (so no line password needed), service pw-enc covers any residual plaintext.

Exam patterns:

  • 'Which is more secure for privileged mode?' = enable secret (always).
  • 'Which command hashes the password?' = the one with secret keyword.
  • 'Which produces plaintext storage?' = enable password or username X password (without secret).

hack.memory: SECRET > PASSWORD. Always. enable secret (not enable password). username X secret Y (not username X password Y). secret = hashed. password = plaintext or Type 7 reversible. Cisco exam: the 'secret' option is always the more secure answer.
hack.practice: Audit any Cisco config you have access to. grep for 'password' vs 'secret'. Anywhere you see 'password' followed by plaintext or Type 7, flag it for remediation. Convert to 'secret' with Type 9 where possible.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Universal exam rule: prefer secret. Know the commands that use each keyword. Always pick secret-based answers for 'most secure' questions.

5.3.h.info:
Cisco IOS supports three banner types, each displayed at a different point in the login sequence. The display order is tested frequently on the CCNA.

Display sequence for a connecting user:

  1. MOTD banner — first thing shown. Before any login prompt. Appears on all connections (console, VTY, AUX).
  2. Login banner — shown after MOTD, before the username/password prompt.
  3. (Authentication happens here)
  4. EXEC banner — shown after successful authentication, as the user enters the CLI.

What each banner is for:

  • MOTD (Message of the Day) — intended for legal warnings and announcements visible to ALL connections, even failed ones. The universal location for 'unauthorized access prohibited' notices.
  • Login banner — shown just before credentials are requested. Used for pre-auth notices specific to login (e.g., 'Enter your credentials to continue').
  • EXEC banner — shown only after successful login. Used for post-auth notices: scheduled maintenance windows, current alerts, system state.

Example full sequence:

[Connection established]
----------------------------------------
Authorized users only. Activity monitored.
Violators will be prosecuted.    ← MOTD banner
----------------------------------------
Enter credentials to continue.    ← Login banner
----------------------------------------
Username: admin
Password: ********
----------------------------------------
Maintenance window Sunday 02:00-04:00 UTC.
Current CPU: 42%. Recent alerts: 0.    ← EXEC banner
----------------------------------------
Router>

Mnemonic: M-L-E = MOTD → Login → EXEC. 'My Lawyer Explains.' Or: 'Meets Login Enters' (the flow of a session).

Common exam pattern: 'What order do banners display?' = MOTD first, Login second, EXEC third (after auth).

hack.memory: Order = MOTD → Login → EXEC. 'My Lawyer Explains.' MOTD = everyone (legal notice). Login = pre-auth notice. EXEC = post-auth info. Authentication happens BETWEEN Login and EXEC.
hack.practice: Configure all three: banner motd #Legal warning#, banner login #Enter credentials#, banner exec #Maintenance tonight#. Disconnect/reconnect. Observe MOTD → Login → credentials → EXEC. The order is unmistakable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Guaranteed exam question. M-L-E mnemonic. MOTD is before any auth; EXEC is after successful auth.

5.3.h.info:
The MOTD banner serves a legal purpose: it establishes that anyone connecting has been warned that access is restricted and activity is monitored. This notice is essential for successful prosecution of unauthorized access under computer-misuse laws (CFAA in the US, similar statutes elsewhere).

What to include in MOTD:

  • Clear statement that access is restricted to authorized users.
  • Notice that activity is monitored and logged.
  • Statement that unauthorized access may result in criminal/civil penalties.
  • Optional: reference to organizational policy or legal code.

Example GOOD MOTD:

banner motd #
****************************************************
   WARNING: Authorized Access Only
   This system is for authorized users.
   All activity is monitored and recorded.
   Unauthorized access is prohibited and
   violators will be prosecuted.
****************************************************
#

What NOT to include in MOTD:

  • 'Welcome' — a defense lawyer can argue 'Welcome' implies invitation, weakening the legal case for prosecution. Don't use it in any security-relevant banner.
  • Device hostname — helps attackers map your infrastructure.
  • Model/IOS version — gives attackers specific CVE targeting info.
  • IP addresses — reveals network topology.
  • Location details — physical security risk and targeting.
  • Organization name (in some cases) — may be necessary for context but can help targeted attacks.

Example BAD MOTD (what not to do):

banner motd #
Welcome to ROUTER01-HQ!
Cisco 4451-X running IOS XE 17.3.5
Located in: Dallas DC Rack 12, IP: 10.1.1.1
Please enjoy your session.
#

This banner hands attackers hostname, model, IOS version, location, and IP — everything needed for a targeted attack — and uses 'Welcome' which undermines prosecution.

Exam pattern: 'What should a MOTD banner contain?' = legal warning. 'What should it NOT contain?' = device info, 'Welcome,' location, IP. 'What word should never appear in a security banner?' = Welcome.

hack.memory: MOTD = LEGAL WARNING for EVERYONE. Include: 'authorized only, monitored, prosecuted.' NEVER include: 'Welcome,' device info, IP, location. 'Welcome' weakens prosecution case (implies invitation). Device info helps attackers.
hack.practice: Audit your org's banners (if any). Check for 'Welcome,' model numbers, hostnames, IPs. Flag and remove. Replace with a legal warning template (many ISP/university samples online).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests what MOTD should/shouldn't contain. 'Welcome' is the trap answer — never in a security banner.

5.3.h.info:
All Cisco banner commands use a delimiter character to mark the beginning and end of the banner text. The delimiter is ANY character that does not appear in the banner itself. Commonly used: #, $, %, !.

Syntax:

Router(config)# banner motd # text text text #

Or multi-line:

Router(config)# banner motd #
Line one of banner.
Line two of banner.
Line three.
#

The first # marks the start; the second # (on its own line or end of text) marks the end.

Key rules:

  • The delimiter must be a character NOT used in the banner text.
  • If your banner contains #, use a different delimiter (e.g., $).
  • Case matters — lowercase and uppercase are different characters.
  • Common delimiter choice: # for most banners, $ when # appears in the text.

All three banner commands follow the same pattern:

Router(config)# banner motd # Warning message #
Router(config)# banner login # Enter credentials #
Router(config)# banner exec # System info #

Special characters in banners:

  • $(hostname) — inserts the device hostname at runtime. Be cautious — exposing hostname in banners is a security concern.
  • $(domain) — inserts the IP domain name.
  • $(line) — inserts the line number (con 0, vty 3, etc.).
  • These are tokens, not literal dollar-signs. They're expanded when the banner displays.

Exam pattern: know the delimiter concept — any character not in the banner text marks start/end. The # is common but not required.

hack.memory: banner motd # text # — the # is a DELIMITER, not required to be #. Any character not in the text works. Multi-line: put the end delimiter on its own line. Tokens: $(hostname), $(domain), $(line) — but be careful exposing hostnames.
hack.practice: Configure a multi-line MOTD banner: 'banner motd $' then type several lines, end with '$' on its own line. Verify with 'show run | section banner'. Try using 'banner motd #Welcome # Warning#' — fails because # is in the text.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Know delimiter concept. Exam may test syntax: banner TYPE DELIM text DELIM. The delimiter is any unused character.

5.3.i.info:
security passwords min-length [0-16] is a global configuration command that enforces a minimum password length for all passwords configured on the device AFTER the command is enabled. It applies to enable passwords/secrets, line passwords, and local usernames.

Syntax:

Router(config)# security passwords min-length 10

This rejects any new password shorter than 10 characters.

Valid range: 0 to 16 characters. Cisco and most security standards recommend a minimum of 10 characters. Setting min-length 0 disables enforcement (not recommended).

What it affects:

  • enable password / enable secret — new values rejected if too short.
  • username X password Y / username X secret Y — new users rejected if password too short.
  • Line passwords (password X under line config).
  • Any command that takes a password argument.

Error behavior: attempting a too-short password produces:

Router(config)# security passwords min-length 10
Router(config)# enable secret abc
% Password too short - must be at least 10 characters. Password configuration failed

Some IOS versions may warn rather than reject — behavior can vary by platform and image. Combine with other controls for robust enforcement.

What it does NOT do:

  • Does NOT enforce complexity (mixed case, numbers, symbols). IOS has no native complexity enforcement — that requires AAA server policy.
  • Does NOT retroactively change or reject existing passwords. Only applies going forward.
  • Does NOT enforce maximum length, rotation, or history — those are separate controls (often handled by AAA).

Pairs well with: enable secret (hashing), service password-encryption (Type 7 for any residual plaintext), AAA server for complexity/rotation/history.

Exam pattern: 'What command enforces minimum password length?' = security passwords min-length X. 'Does it affect existing passwords?' = No, prospective only.

hack.memory: security passwords min-length N = reject new passwords shorter than N. Range 0-16. Recommended 10+. NOT retroactive — existing short passwords still work. Does NOT enforce complexity (that needs AAA). Global command.
hack.practice: Lab: 'security passwords min-length 10'. Try 'enable secret abc' → rejected (too short). Try 'enable secret LongPassword1!' → accepted. Existing short passwords remain — confirm with show run.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam knows this command exactly. Syntax, range (0-16), and prospective-only enforcement.

5.3.i.info:
Critical behavioral note: security passwords min-length is prospective only. It enforces the minimum on new or changed passwords after the command is entered. It does NOT retroactively reject, change, or require update of existing passwords.

Example scenario:

Router(config)# enable secret abc             ! Existing short password, stays
Router(config)# security passwords min-length 10
Router(config)# enable secret newer           ! Still short, REJECTED
Router(config)# enable secret NewLongerOne    ! Accepted

The original abc password is still active. Admins can log in with it. The min-length check only kicks in when changing the password.

What this means operationally:

  • Enabling min-length does NOT force a password reset for existing users. You must manually re-enter each password.
  • Audit scripts should verify BOTH: (a) the min-length command is configured, AND (b) no current password is shorter than the minimum.
  • Rolling out min-length across a fleet should be paired with a password-rotation campaign to bring existing passwords into compliance.

Why Cisco designed it this way:

  • Avoids immediately locking admins out when the command is enabled.
  • Allows gradual migration to stronger password policies.
  • Consistent with other IOS security commands that take effect prospectively (e.g., login block-for, ACL changes).

Contrast with some modern password systems: Azure AD, Okta, and similar can force password changes on next login when policy changes. IOS min-length does not — you must trigger the changes manually or via automation.

Operational workflow to fully comply:

  1. Enable security passwords min-length 10.
  2. Script-audit all passwords against the minimum.
  3. Force rotation on any short password.
  4. Verify via audit that no password < 10 chars remains.

Exam pattern: 'Does min-length enforce retroactively?' = No. 'Does changing min-length require existing users to reset?' = No (not automatically).

states: Only NEW pw must meet minimum

hack.memory: min-length = PROSPECTIVE only. Existing short passwords stay active. Only NEW or CHANGED passwords get checked. If you want full compliance, you must manually rotate existing passwords too — the command alone isn't enough.
hack.practice: Lab: set 'enable secret abc' (3 chars). Then 'security passwords min-length 10'. Log out, log back in with 'abc' — still works. Try to change secret to 'short' — rejected. Confirms prospective-only behavior.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam may ask: does the command affect existing passwords? Answer: NO. It's prospective-only.

5.3.j.info:
login block-for is a global configuration command that provides brute-force login protection by temporarily blocking all login attempts after too many consecutive failures within a defined window. This defends VTY/console against automated password-guessing attacks.

Full syntax:

Router(config)# login block-for [seconds] attempts [count] within [seconds]

Three parameters, all required, in this exact order:

  • block-for [seconds] — how long to block login attempts when triggered.
  • attempts [count] — number of failed logins needed to trigger.
  • within [seconds] — time window for counting failures.

Concrete example:

Router(config)# login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60

Meaning: if 3 failed login attempts occur within 60 seconds, block ALL logins for 120 seconds.

Quiet mode: the blocking period is called quiet mode. During quiet mode, IOS refuses all login attempts — including legitimate ones. To prevent locking yourself out, configure a whitelist ACL:

Router(config)# ip access-list standard ADMIN_IPS
Router(config-std-nacl)# permit 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255
Router(config)# login quiet-mode access-class ADMIN_IPS

IPs matching the ACL can still log in during quiet mode. This is your escape hatch.

Supplementary commands:

  • login delay 3 — enforces a 3-second delay between consecutive login attempts (slows brute force).
  • login on-failure log — generates a syslog message on failed login.
  • login on-success log — generates a syslog message on successful login.

Typical hardened VTY config:

login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60
login quiet-mode access-class ADMIN_IPS
login on-failure log
login on-success log
login delay 3

This combo massively slows brute-force attacks while preserving admin access from approved IPs.

hack.memory: login block-for [LOCK SEC] attempts [N] within [WINDOW SEC]. 'Block for 120 if 3 fails within 60.' Quiet mode = lockout period. Quiet-mode access-class = admin escape hatch. Add 'login delay,' 'login on-failure log' for full hardening.
hack.practice: Lab: 'login block-for 60 attempts 3 within 30'. SSH in and deliberately fail 3 times quickly. Try to SSH a 4th time — blocked for 60 seconds. Add 'login quiet-mode access-class' with your management IP permitted and verify you can still log in.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam testable. Know exact syntax and parameter order.

5.3.j.info:
The canonical example for memorizing the syntax: login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60.

English translation: if 3 failed login attempts happen within a 60-second window, block ALL login attempts for the next 120 seconds.

Why these numbers are typical:

  • block-for 120 (seconds) = 2-minute lockout. Long enough to deter automated brute-force, short enough that a legitimate admin fat-fingering three times doesn't wait 10 minutes.
  • attempts 3 = 3 failures trigger. A real user who mistypes 3x is probably confused or lookups; 3 failures in a short window is suspicious.
  • within 60 (seconds) = 1-minute counting window. Fast enough to catch brute-force but ignore slow accidental failures over hours.

Rate-limiting math: at these settings, a brute-force attacker gets 3 guesses per 2 minutes + 60 seconds = roughly 3 guesses per 3 minutes. That's 60 guesses per hour, or 1,440 per day. Even a 6-character password with only letters has ~300 million combinations. At 1,440/day, it would take ~200,000 days = 570 years to brute force. Completely infeasible.

Combined with login delay 3: each successful attempt also takes 3 seconds minimum, further slowing attack.

Balancing considerations:

  • Too strict (e.g., 1 failure locks for 10 minutes): admins will lock themselves out repeatedly.
  • Too lenient (e.g., 100 attempts locks for 5 seconds): provides no real protection.
  • 120/3/60 is a reasonable default. Adjust based on your threat model.

With quiet-mode ACL: during the 120-second lockout, admins from the ACL can still log in. Essential for operations.

Exam memorization: practice typing the full command from memory. Cisco may ask the EXACT syntax and parameter order.

hack.memory: Canonical: '120 attempts 3 within 60' = block 120s after 3 fails in 60s. Cuts brute-force to ~60 guesses/hour — infeasible for any real password. Add quiet-mode ACL so admins aren't locked out.
hack.practice: Recite the command 5 times without looking: 'login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60.' Then type it in Packet Tracer. Verify with 'show login' — see parameters listed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Highly exam-testable. Exact syntax and numbers may be asked.

5.3.j.info:
The three parameters of login block-for must appear in a specific order: block-for → attempts → within. IOS will reject the command if parameters are out of sequence.

Correct:

login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60

Incorrect (IOS rejects):

login attempts 3 block-for 120 within 60          ! WRONG ORDER
login within 60 attempts 3 block-for 120          ! WRONG ORDER
login block-for 120 within 60 attempts 3          ! WRONG ORDER

Mnemonic to remember the order:

  • Block-for — how long to block (first, because it's the consequence).
  • Attempts — how many fails trigger it.
  • Within — the time window for counting.

BAW or Bad Attempts Within. Or think of it as a sentence: "Block FOR [time] after [N] ATTEMPTS WITHIN [window]."

Default values if parameters are missing: IOS does not accept partial syntax. All three parameters must be specified.

Companion commands:

  • login delay [seconds] — inter-attempt delay. Not part of block-for syntax.
  • login quiet-mode access-class [ACL] — quiet-mode escape ACL.
  • login on-failure log [every N] — syslog for failures.
  • login on-success log [every N] — syslog for successes.

Verifying the config:

Router# show login
     A login delay of 3 seconds is applied.
     All failed login is logged.
     Router enabled to watch for login Attacks.
     If more than 3 login failures occur in 60 seconds or less,
     logins will be disabled for 120 seconds.

     Router presently in Normal-Mode.

Exam pattern: 'What is the correct syntax for login block-for?' = block-for X attempts Y within Z. Know the order or you lose the point.

hack.memory: Order: BLOCK-FOR → ATTEMPTS → WITHIN. BAW. 'Block FOR 120 seconds after 3 ATTEMPTS WITHIN 60 seconds.' Read it as a sentence. IOS rejects any other order.
hack.practice: Type the command in 5 different orders. Only one is accepted. Verify with 'show login.' This muscle memory prevents exam mistakes.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam checks exact syntax and parameter order. BAW mnemonic.
